mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Slovenia on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe. Slovenia sits at the meeting point of the Alps, the Mediterranean, and the Balkans, bordered by Italy, Austria, Hungary and Croatia 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Slovenia?
mapQuestions.answer: Ljubljana, in the centre. About 280,000 people, pronounced "lyoob-lyah-nah". A small, green, walkable, river-running-through-it kind of capital.

mapQuestions.question: What language does Slovenia speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Slovenian (slovenščina), a Slavic language. Slovenia is unusual in that the language even has a "dual" form for talking about exactly two people, on top of singular and plural.

mapQuestions.question: How is Slovenia geographically lucky?
mapQuestions.answer: It is one of the only countries in Europe with snowy Alps, a Mediterranean coast, big lakes, deep cave systems, and rolling vineyards, ALL within a 2-hour drive.

mapQuestions.question: When did Slovenia become its own country?
mapQuestions.answer: 1991. Slovenia was part of Yugoslavia from 1918 to 1991, and the first part to break away peacefully (in a "Ten-Day War" with very few casualties). Today it is a member of the EU and uses the Euro.

foods.name: Potica
foods.description: A rolled-up sweet bread with walnut, tarragon or poppy-seed filling, baked in a fluted ring tin. The Slovenian celebration cake, eaten at every Christmas, Easter and wedding.

foods.name: Jota
foods.description: A thick hearty soup of sauerkraut, beans, potato and smoked pork. Karst winter food, originally a peasant dish.

foods.name: Štruklji
foods.description: Rolled dumplings stuffed with cottage cheese, walnuts, tarragon or apples, boiled or steamed, served sweet or savoury.

foods.name: Kremšnita
foods.description: A square of vanilla custard and whipped cream sandwiched between layers of crispy puff pastry, dusted with icing sugar. Bled is famous for it.

foods.name: Slovenian honey
foods.description: Slovenia is one of the world's top beekeeping countries, with its own native bee (the Carniolan bee). Honey here is treated almost as a national treasure.

foods.name: Soča River trout
foods.description: A pink-spotted river trout that only lives in the turquoise Soča River in western Slovenia. Often grilled simply with butter and lemon.

scavengerItems.label: Lake Bled with the church on a tiny island and a castle on a clifftop
The Julian Alps with Mount Triglav, the national symbol
Lipizzaner horses (the original snow-white show horses come from Slovenia)
The bright turquoise Soča River winding through gorges
A limestone cave (Postojna, Škocjan, or many others)
The Adriatic coast at Piran (a Venetian-style red-roofed town)
A traditional painted Slovenian beehive (with folk-art panels)
Cyclists on the Ljubljana riverside paths
Dense beech and spruce forest covering 60% of the country
A dragon statue on the Dragon Bridge in Ljubljana (the city's symbol)
The "human fish" (a pale blind cave salamander) at Postojna Caves
A small wooden Carantanian hilltop church

geoMatches.description: The capital. Small, green, with the Ljubljanica river winding through the centre, a castle on the hill, and dragon statues on the bridges.
Slovenia's most photographed spot. A glacial lake with a tiny island in the middle (with a church), a medieval clifftop castle, and the Julian Alps behind.
The only national park in Slovenia, covering the Julian Alps and Mount Triglav (2,864 m). Hiking, gorges, waterfalls, alpine meadows.
One of the largest tourist-accessible cave systems in Europe. Ride a small underground train into 24 km of chambers, stalactites and the famous "human fish".
A small Venetian-influenced town on Slovenia's tiny 47 km Adriatic coast. Red-tiled roofs, narrow alleys, salt pans nearby.
The valley of the bright turquoise Soča River in the west. White-water rafting, hiking, and the moving outdoor museum of the WWI Isonzo Front.

senses.smell: Pine forest in Triglav, baked potica with walnut, wood-fire smoke in mountain villages, salty Adriatic air at Piran, fresh river spray on the Soča…
senses.hear: "Dober dan!" greetings, church bells echoing across alpine valleys, the soft clop of an oar at Lake Bled, the rush of the Soča, bee hives buzzing in spring…
senses.taste: Sweet walnut potica, rich kremšnita with vanilla cream, fresh river trout, sharp Slovenian forest honey, warm strudel from a mountain hut…
senses.feel: The cold of an alpine lake on a summer afternoon, soft snow at Vogel ski resort, smooth Soča pebbles, the chill of a deep cave, the warm sun on Piran's red rooftops…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is Lake Bled the most postcard place in Europe?
stories.body: Lake Bled is small (only 2 km across), but it has every fairytale ingredient in one frame: a glassy green lake; a tiny island RIGHT in the middle with a 17th-century church on it; a medieval castle on a 130-metre clifftop above the water; the snowy Julian Alps in the background. Visitors can row a wooden "pletna" boat (rowed by a single oarsman, a tradition for 7 generations of the same families) out to the island, climb the 99 steps to the church, and ring the church bell (a tradition for good luck). Bled is also where Yugoslavia's leader Tito had his summer villa. Today it is one of the most-visited spots in Slovenia, and you can't take a bad photo of it.

stories.question: What is a "human fish"?
stories.body: In the dark cave waters of Slovenia lives the "proteus" (or "olm"), a pale, eyeless, finger-sized salamander that lives ENTIRELY in cave water. It has translucent pink skin (no need for skin pigment in the dark), tiny vestigial eyes, external feathery gills, and can live up to 100 years. In the 1600s, when locals saw them washed out of caves after floods, they thought they had found baby dragons. Today they are called "human fish" in Slovenia ("človeška ribica") because their pale skin looks human. You can see them in tanks at Postojna Caves. They are one of the most extraordinary cave-adapted animals on Earth.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A creature spends 100 years in darkness without ever seeing daylight. What would your life be like if you lived like that?

stories.question: Why are Slovenians such mad beekeepers?
stories.body: Slovenia has one of the highest densities of beekeepers per person in Europe: about 1 in every 200 Slovenians keeps bees. The country is the home of the "Carniolan bee", a particularly gentle bee subspecies that is now used in beehives around the world. Slovenia has a strong national tradition of painting beehive front panels with folk-art scenes (saints, hunters, dancing animals, mini-paintings used to help individual bees identify their own hive). Beekeeping is so important in Slovenia that the country lobbied the UN to declare 20 May (the birthday of the great Slovenian beekeeper Anton Janša) "World Bee Day" in 2018. It worked. The day is now official.

stories.question: Why is one mountain on the flag?
stories.body: Mount Triglav (2,864 m), the highest peak in Slovenia, is the country's deepest national symbol. The triple peak is on the Slovenian flag, the coat of arms, the Euro coins. There is an old Slovenian tradition that every Slovenian should climb Triglav at least once in their lifetime, and many actually do (with sturdy boots and a guide). The mountain was once thought to be home to the legendary "Zlatorog" (the golden-horned chamois), a magical mountain goat at the heart of Slovenian folklore. Triglav National Park surrounds it, protecting some of the best-preserved alpine landscapes in Europe.

stories.question: What happened on the Soča front?
stories.body: During World War I, the bright-turquoise Soča River in western Slovenia was the front line between Italy and the Austro-Hungarian Empire for two and a half years (1915 to 1917). Over a million soldiers died here in 12 separate battles. Today the valley is one of the most beautiful and peaceful places in Europe, with rafting, hiking, alpine meadows. But everywhere you walk, there are old trenches, tunnels, war cemeteries, war museums, and the memory of the Isonzo Front. Ernest Hemingway drove ambulances here in 1918 and later wrote "A Farewell to Arms" based on what he saw.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Slovenia so far?
Lake, mountain, cave, coast, Ljubljana, which has felt the most "Slovenia"?
Did you ring the Lake Bled church bell? Did you make a wish?
How is Slovenia different from Italy or Austria, which are right next door?
Slovenia has snow Alps, the Mediterranean coast, and big caves, all in a country the size of Wales. How does that change how it feels to travel here?

wordSearch.hint: Slovenian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Brown bear
animals.description: Slovenia has one of the largest brown bear populations in Europe (about 1,000 bears, mostly in the south).

animals.name: Grey wolf
animals.description: Returning to the Slovenian forests after near-extinction in the 20th century. Mostly in the Notranjska region.

animals.name: Eurasian lynx
animals.description: About 100 lynx live in Slovenian forests, especially the Kočevski Rog. Hard to see but their tracks turn up in winter snow.

animals.name: Lipizzaner horse
animals.description: The white show-horse breed originally from the Slovenian village of Lipica, dating back to 1580. Still bred at the Lipica Stud Farm today.

animals.name: Carniolan honeybee
animals.description: A particularly gentle native bee. Slovenia is one of the most beekeeping-mad countries on Earth.

animals.name: Olm (proteus)
animals.description: The "human fish": a pale, eyeless, finger-sized cave salamander that lives only in Slovenian and Balkan cave waters. Can live 100+ years.

animals.name: Golden eagle
animals.description: Nests in the Julian Alps and Triglav National Park. About 70 pairs in the country.
